summary: 8b has two sections. First, it completes the baraita distinguishing the YK kohein gadol (separation for holiness, other priests may touch him) from the para priest (separation for purity, other priests must not touch him). R. Yosei b'R. Chanina challenges: if the YK kohein gadol is only required to be sprinkled on day 3 and day 7, why is he sprinkled on day 4 at all - day 4 cannot be day 3 or day 7 of any tumah! The Gemara answers: sprinkling is a shvut (Rabbinic, not overriding Shabbat), so we actually sprinkle on '7 minus Shabbat.' Rava resolves: the YK kohein gadol's separation date depends on the calendar (3 Tishrei each year, regardless); the para priest's date is chosen by us (we schedule it so the 4th day of his separation falls on Shabbat, preventing his hazaah then and keeping him to minimum required sprinklings). Second, the Mishnah's 'Lishkat Parhedrin' (Chamber of Persian Governors) is explained: R. Yehuda says it was originally called 'Lishkat Balvati' (Chamber of Nobles); it was renamed 'Parhedrin' because the corrupt Second Temple High Priests changed yearly like Persian governors who serve 12-month terms. A tangential discussion about bakers (nachtomin) and their exemption from separating maaser sheni is introduced.

display.title: YK kohein gadol vs. para priest separation; Rava's calendar solution
display.oneLine: The baraita's distinction between the two priests is analyzed, R. Yosei b'R. Chanina objects that day 4 is impossible for any tumah-day, and Rava solves the problem by showing that the para priest's separation date is within our control - so we schedule it to avoid the impossible day.
display.shortSummary: The baraita says the ONLY difference between the para priest and the YK kohein gadol is: the para priest's separation is for purity (other priests may not touch him); the YK kohein gadol's separation is for holiness (other priests may touch him). R. Yosei b'R. Chanina challenges: if the YK kohein gadol is sprinkled only on day 3 and day 7, why does the Mishnah say he is sprinkled for the whole 7 days? Day 4 cannot be day 3 or day 7 of any prior tumah - so sprinkling on day 4 is unnecessary. The Gemara counters: 'all 7 days' minus Shabbat (since hazaah is a shvut that does not override Shabbat). But day 4 still seems unnecessary. Rava's solution: the YK kohein gadol's separation is fixed by calendar - he must be separated on 3 Tishrei every year regardless. Sometimes his 4th day of separation falls on Shabbat - that day is skipped. But the para priest's separation date is chosen by us. We schedule the para priest's separation so that his 4th day (the impossible day) falls on Shabbat - and he also skips that day. Result: para priest is also sprinkled only on the relevant days (3, 5, 6, 7, etc.), never on day 4.
display.whats: Rava's solution hinges on a crucial distinction: things that are in our control (biddayn talya milta) vs. things determined by external factors (calendar, calendar-fixed rituals). The para priest's separation is in our hands, so we plan it to be efficient. The YK kohein gadol's separation is calendar-fixed - we accept whatever happens.
display.hint: Why is day 4 of the YK kohein gadol's separation necessarily sprinkled even though it is not a possible day-3 or day-7? Because his separation starts on 3 Tishrei by necessity and sometimes his day 4 is not Shabbat. Rava shows we cannot avoid this for him, but we can avoid it for the para priest.

learning.learnerQuestion: What makes Rava's solution work? Why can we schedule the para priest but not the YK kohein gadol?
learning.coreTension: Both the YK kohein gadol and the para priest face the same theoretical problem: why is sprinkling needed on day 4 (which cannot possibly be day 3 or day 7 of any tumah)? The answer must differentiate them. Rava sees the key: the para priest's start date is our choice; the YK kohein gadol's is fixed by calendar (3 Tishrei). We choose the para priest's start so his day 4 is Shabbat, when hazaah is forbidden. The YK kohein gadol's day 4 cannot always be avoided.
learning.coreMove: The principle 'biddayn talya milta' (the matter depends on us) vs. 'b'kevi'a d'yar'cha talya milta' (the matter depends on the calendar) distinguishes the two cases. This is a scheduling solution, not a halachic change.
learning.resolution: Rava's ruling: separate the YK kohein gadol on 3 Tishrei each year (no matter what day of the week it falls on). Separate the para priest on the fourth day of the week - so that his 4th day falls on Shabbat, the hazaah-free day.

takeaway.text: Halachic problems can sometimes be solved through scheduling rather than legal reinterpretation. When we control the variables (when to start a process), we can engineer the outcome to match the ideal halachic result.

reasoningPattern.notes: A recurring Talmudic move: distinguish cases where humans control the timing (and can optimize) from cases where the calendar is fixed (and we must accept what comes). The para priest vs. YK kohein gadol distinction is a clean example.

learning.ahaMoment: Hazaah is a shvut - it cannot override Shabbat. This seemingly minor technical detail is what allows Rava to solve the problem: the one day we can never sprinkle (Shabbat) is the one day we should schedule the para priest's 'impossible day' to fall on.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may think Rava is saying 'we never sprinkle on day 4.' He is not - for the YK kohein gadol, day 4 is sprinkled (it may be needed in some years when his start date and day 4 don't land on Shabbat). For the para priest, we engineer it so day 4 is Shabbat. These are different rules.
learning.memoryAnchor: YK kohein gadol: calendar-fixed (3 Tishrei), accept whatever comes. Para priest: we choose (separate on 4th day of week so day 4 lands on Shabbat and is automatically skipped). Shvut saves the day - literally.

argumentFlow.label: Baraita: only difference is holiness vs. purity separation
argumentFlow.speaker: Baraita
argumentFlow.text: There is only one difference between the priest who burns the para aduma and the High Priest on Yom Kippur: the para priest's separation is for purity (other priests may not touch him); the YK kohein gadol's separation is for holiness (other priests may touch him).

argumentFlow.label: R. Yosei b'R. Chanina: day 4 sprinkling is impossible for the YK kohein gadol
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yosei b'R. Chanina
argumentFlow.text: Granted, the Mishnah says the kohein gadol is sprinkled on days 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7 (to cover possible day-3 or day-7 of prior tumah). But why sprinkle on day 4 at all? Day 4 cannot be day 3 of any prior tumah (he has been separated for 4 days) and cannot be day 7 either. So why does the Mishnah imply day 4 is sprinkled?

argumentFlow.label: Hazaah is a shvut - it does not override Shabbat - so 'all 7' means 7 minus Shabbat
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Hazaah is a Rabbinic prohibition on Shabbat (shvut) - it cannot be performed on Shabbat. So when the Mishnah says 'all seven days,' it means 'seven minus any Shabbat that falls among them.' If Shabbat falls on day 4, day 4 is automatically skipped.

sourceRefs.note: Hazaah as shvut explains 'all seven minus Shabbat'

argumentFlow.label: Rava: YK kohein gadol and para priest differ in scheduling control
argumentFlow.speaker: Rava
argumentFlow.text: Therefore: the YK kohein gadol's separation depends on the calendar (he must be separated on 3 Tishrei, whatever day of the week that is). Whenever 3 Tishrei comes, we separate him - and accept that sometimes his day 4 falls on Shabbat (skipped) and sometimes it does not. The para priest's separation depends on us. We separate him on the 4th day of the week, so that his 4th day of separation falls on Shabbat - and the impossible day is automatically omitted.

concepts.definition: A Rabbinic prohibition on Shabbat - an action not biblically forbidden on Shabbat but forbidden by Rabbinic decree. Hazaah (sprinkling) is a shvut and does not override Shabbat.
The matter depends on us - when we control the timing of a process, we can schedule it optimally. Contrasted with calendar-fixed dates that depend on external factors.

visualizableElements.label: 7-day separation calendar for para priest
visualizableElements.description: 7-day horizontal timeline showing days 1-7 of the para priest's separation when begun on Wednesday (day 4 of week). Day 4 of separation = Shabbat. Hazaah on days 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7. Day 4 (Shabbat) = no hazaah, skipped. Contrast with YK kohein gadol whose day 4 cannot always be arranged this way.

visualizableElements.label: Calendar-fixed vs. scheduling-flexible
visualizableElements.description: Two-column comparison: YK kohein gadol (separation fixed to 3 Tishrei, accept whatever day-of-week it is) vs. para priest (we choose the start day, so we pick Wednesday to make day 4 fall on Shabbat).

quizSeeds.q: Why is day 4 of the separation 'impossible' for the purpose of hazaah?
quizSeeds.a: Day 4 cannot be day 3 of any prior tumah (the priest has already been separated 4 days, so his last possible tumah was before day 1, making day 3 of that tumah = day 3 of separation, not day 4). It also cannot be day 7 (day 7 of tumah would be day 7 of separation, not day 4).

quizSeeds.q: What is Rava's solution to the day-4 problem for the para priest?
quizSeeds.a: Separate the para priest on the 4th day of the week (Wednesday), so his 4th day of separation falls on Shabbat. Since hazaah is forbidden on Shabbat (shvut), day 4 is automatically skipped without any special ruling.

quizSeeds.q: Why can this scheduling solution not be applied to the YK kohein gadol?
quizSeeds.a: The YK kohein gadol's separation is fixed by the calendar (3 Tishrei each year). We cannot choose when 3 Tishrei falls - so we accept whatever day his day 4 ends up being.

misconceptions.wrong: Rava says the para priest is never sprinkled on day 4.
misconceptions.right: Rava says we schedule the para priest's separation so day 4 falls on Shabbat, when hazaah is forbidden as a shvut. Day 4 is not skipped by ruling but by Shabbat coincidence engineered through scheduling.

relatedSugyot.reason: The hazaah dispute among R. Meir/R. Yosei/R. Chanina Segan that this section concludes
Immediately following: the Mishnah's Lishkat Parhedrin term is explained

sugyot.finalRuling: Practical: the para priest's separation begins on Wednesday (day 4 of the week) so day 4 of separation falls on Shabbat. The YK kohein gadol is separated on 3 Tishrei each year.
requiresUnderstanding: Rava's calendar solution is best understood after reading 8a's hazaah dispute
What shvut means as a category of Rabbinic Shabbat prohibition

display.title: Lishkat Parhedrin: from nobles' chamber to rotating governors; bakers and tithes
display.oneLine: The Mishnah's term 'Lishkat Parhedrin' is explained as a renamed version of 'Lishkat Balvati' (nobles' chamber), reflecting Second Temple corruption where the High Priesthood was sold and replaced annually; a tangential discussion about bakers' tithe obligations follows.
display.shortSummary: A baraita: R. Yehuda asks, was this really a 'Lishkat Parhedrin' (chamber of Persian governors)? It was actually 'Lishkat Balvati' (chamber of nobles/senators). The explanation: in the early Second Temple period, during the era of Shimon HaTzaddik, it was called Lishkat Balvati. Later, when the High Priesthood was sold for money and the High Priest was replaced every 12 months (like Persian parhedrin who rotate annually), the room was renamed to reflect this corruption. A completely separate Mishnah-referencing discussion follows: bakers (nachtomin) are exempt from separating maaser sheni on their dough. The Gemara explains why: they do not need to separate trumah gedolah (already done by the thresher), maaser rishon and maaser ani (burden of proof falls on the claimant), or maaser sheni (the Gemara proceeds to explain this in 9a).
display.whats: The Lishkat Parhedrin section is an aggadic/historical aside: the room's name encodes the tragedy of Second Temple corruption. The rotating-governors metaphor is biting - the High Priesthood became a political appointment with a one-year term. The bakers' discussion is a transition to 9a's Yochanan High Priest material.
display.hint: The room's name changed because the institution it housed changed. The name Parhedrin is not honorific - it is a damning comparison.

learning.learnerQuestion: Why is the room called Parhedrin? Is this supposed to be flattering to the High Priest?
learning.coreTension: The original name 'Balvati' implied noble, permanent occupants. 'Parhedrin' implies rotating annual appointments - like Persian bureaucrats who never stay long enough to build anything. The name change is not neutral: it is a running critique embedded in the Temple's architecture.
learning.coreMove: R. Yehuda's question ('was it really called Parhedrin?') invites the honest answer: no, it was called that by the people as dark humor/social commentary on the corrupt practice of selling and rotating the High Priesthood.
learning.resolution: The practical halacha about bakers' tithes continues in 9a with Yochanan High Priest's decree on dema'i tithes.

takeaway.text: Temple institutions are not just halachic constructs - they encode historical memory. The name 'Parhedrin' preserved in the Mishnah is itself a form of mourning for what the High Priesthood became in the late Second Temple period.

reasoningPattern.notes: Talmudic discussions of place names often double as historical or ethical commentary. 'Balvati' (honored/permanent) vs. 'Parhedrin' (rotating/corrupt) is a case where etymology encodes judgment.

learning.ahaMoment: The same physical room had two names across history: one reflecting its proper purpose (housing the honored High Priest), one reflecting its degraded reality (housing a politically-appointed annual replacement). The Mishnah preserved both.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may miss that R. Yehuda is not disputing whether the room existed but what it should have been called. His question is rhetorical - he knows the name changed and is leading to the explanation.
learning.memoryAnchor: Lishkat Balvati (noble senators) -> renamed Parhedrin (annual rotating governors) when the High Priesthood was sold for money and changed every 12 months. Embedded in the Mishnah's language is Second Temple history's tragedy.

argumentFlow.label: R. Yehuda: was it really 'Lishkat Parhedrin'?
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yehuda
argumentFlow.text: Was this room really called 'Lishkat Parhedrin' (Chamber of Persian Governors)? It was actually called 'Lishkat Balvati' (Chamber of Nobles)!

sourceRefs.note: R. Yehuda's question about the room's name

argumentFlow.label: Historical explanation: renamed due to corruption
argumentFlow.speaker: Baraita
argumentFlow.text: Originally it was called Lishkat Balvati during the era of Shimon HaTzaddik. But when the Second Temple High Priests began paying for the position and were replaced every 12 months (like the Persian parhedrin who rotate annually), the room was sardonically renamed Lishkat Parhedrin.

concepts.definition: Persian governors who served one-year rotating terms. The term was applied to Second Temple High Priests who were politically appointed and replaced annually, mocking the degradation of the office.
Chamber of Nobles (from Latin 'bouleutai', senators/nobles). The original name of the chamber housing the High Priest, from the era of Shimon HaTzaddik when the High Priesthood was held with dignity.

Produce of doubtful tithe status - purchased from an am ha'aretz who may or may not have separated tithes. Rabbinic stringencies applied to dema'i differ from those for definite tevel (untithed produce).

visualizableElements.label: Balvati vs. Parhedrin era
visualizableElements.description: Timeline showing Shimon HaTzaddik era (Lishkat Balvati, stable High Priesthood, long terms) contrasted with later Second Temple era (Lishkat Parhedrin, annual replacements, purchased office). The same room, two histories.

visualizableElements.label: High Priest as parhedar
visualizableElements.description: Visual of a Persian governor rotating out after 12 months - used as an analogy for the Second Temple High Priests who were replaced annually. The comparison is critical, not honorific.

quizSeeds.q: Why was the chamber originally called 'Lishkat Balvati' and what did the name 'Parhedrin' imply?
quizSeeds.a: 'Balvati' means nobles/senators - a dignified permanent title. 'Parhedrin' refers to Persian governors who rotate annually. The name changed to reflect that Second Temple High Priests were sold the office and replaced every 12 months like rotating bureaucrats.

quizSeeds.q: During whose era was the room called Lishkat Balvati?
quizSeeds.a: During the era of Shimon HaTzaddik, when the High Priesthood was still held with integrity and the High Priest served for many years.

misconceptions.wrong: 'Parhedrin' is an honorary title recognizing the High Priest's importance.
misconceptions.right: 'Parhedrin' is a critical term - it compares the corrupt Second Temple High Priests to Persian bureaucrats who rotate out every year. The name is embedded social criticism.

relatedSugyot.reason: Immediately following: bakers and tithes transition leads into 9a
Second Temple High Priest corruption theme continues with the 300+ kohanim gedolim calculation

requiresUnderstanding: Basic Second Temple history: Shimon HaTzaddik as a defining era

display.title: Bakers and tithe obligations: a transitional passage leading into 9a
display.oneLine: The Gemara establishes why bakers are not required to separate maaser sheni from dema'i grain, setting up the full explanation on 9a via Yochanan High Priest's decree.
display.shortSummary: A Mishnah-citing discussion: bakers who are chaverim and buy grain from amei ha'aretz are not required to separate maaser sheni from dema'i. The Gemara walks through the tiers: trumah gedolah (Yochanan's investigation proved it was already separated), maaser rishon and maaser ani (burden of proof on the Levi/poor claimant - they must prove the baker did not separate), and maaser sheni (explanation given in 9a via Ulla's parhedrin-pressure account).
display.whats: This section bridges the Lishkat Parhedrin material with the 9a opening. It introduces the dema'i tithe framework as a practical halachic question about a specific profession: bakers who are themselves Torah-observant (chaverim) but must buy from amei ha'aretz.
display.hint: Why are bakers specifically singled out? Their commercial reality (continuous sales, government price pressure) makes it impossible to track tithe status of every batch. The Rabbis tailored the requirement to the profession.

learning.learnerQuestion: Why does the Gemara go into bakers' tithe obligations right after the Lishkat Parhedrin discussion?
learning.coreTension: The transition seems abrupt. But both passages involve 'parhedrin': the rotating High Priests (historical/aggadic) and the government officials (parhedrin) who pressured bakers economically (halachic). The word 'parhedrin' links them structurally.
learning.coreMove: The burden-of-proof principle ('hamotzi m'chaveiro alav hara'ayah') is applied to each tier of tithes. For maaser rishon and maaser ani: the Levi/poor person must prove the grain was untithed; they cannot. Therefore no separation required from the baker.
learning.resolution: Bakers separate only trumat maaser and challah. The full explanation for maaser sheni exemption (government pressure by parhedrin enforcers) comes in 9a via Ulla.

takeaway.text: Halachic obligations are calibrated to professional realities. Bakers sell continuously, cannot track each batch's tithe history, and face government economic pressure. The Rabbis recognized these structural constraints when ruling on their tithe obligations.

reasoningPattern.notes: The 'hamotzi m'chaveiro' principle removes the obligation from the baker for two of the tithe tiers. This is a general monetary-law principle applied to a religious obligation context.

learning.ahaMoment: The same word 'parhedrin' (government officials) appears here as the oppressors of bakers, while the Lishkat Parhedrin was named after rotating High Priests. The Talmud's word-play is intentional: both uses reflect institutional corruption and rotation.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may think the baker's exemption means tithes were not required from dema'i at all. In fact, some tithes were required; the exemption is specific to maaser sheni (and explained by economic pressure in 9a).
learning.memoryAnchor: Bakers exempt from dema'i tithes in layers: trumah gedolah (already separated - Yochanan found this), maaser rishon/ani (burden on claimant), maaser sheni (government pressure - see 9a). 'Hamotzi m'chaveiro' clears two tiers; Ulla's parhedrin clears the third.

argumentFlow.label: Bakers exempt from most dema'i tithe obligations
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah/Baraita
argumentFlow.text: Bakers (nachtomin) who purchase grain from amei ha'aretz are not required to separate maaser sheni from dema'i. Only trumat maaser and challah are required from them. The Gemara explains each tithe tier.

argumentFlow.label: Maaser rishon and maaser ani: burden of proof on the claimant
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: For maaser rishon and maaser ani: once grain is separated from tevel status (even in doubt), the Levi or poor person cannot extract their portion without proving the baker did not already separate. 'The one who extracts from another bears the burden of proof' - the claimant must prove the baker owes them.

sourceRefs.note: Burden of proof applied to maaser rishon/ani

argumentFlow.label: Maaser sheni: explanation deferred to 9a
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: For maaser sheni: because Yochanan High Priest sent emissaries throughout Israel (the full explanation continues on 9a with Ulla's account of parhedrin economic pressure on bakers).

concepts.definition: The one who seeks to extract from another bears the burden of proof. Applied here: Levi or poor person claiming maaser from the baker must prove the baker did not already separate it.

visualizableElements.label: Three-tier tithe exemption for bakers
visualizableElements.description: Three-row breakdown: (1) trumah gedolah - already separated per Yochanan's investigation; (2) maaser rishon/ani - burden of proof on claimant; (3) maaser sheni - government pressure (explained on 9a). Shows how each tier is resolved differently.

quizSeeds.q: Why do bakers not need to separate maaser rishon and maaser ani from dema'i grain?
quizSeeds.a: Because the burden of proof is on the claimant (the Levi or poor person). Once grain is separated from strict tevel status, the Levi or poor person must prove the baker did not already separate their portion. Without such proof, the baker owes nothing.

misconceptions.wrong: Bakers are completely exempt from all tithe obligations.
misconceptions.right: Bakers are required to separate trumat maaser (1/100) and challah. The exemptions are specific: trumah gedolah (already separated by thresher), maaser rishon/ani (burden on claimant), and maaser sheni (government pressure - see 9a).

relatedSugyot.reason: 9a immediately explains the maaser sheni exemption via Yochanan High Priest and Ulla's parhedrin-pressure account
Lishkat Parhedrin discussion that immediately precedes this bakers passage

sugyot.finalRuling: Bakers who buy from amei ha'aretz: separate only trumat maaser and challah. No obligation for maaser sheni, maaser rishon, or maaser ani from dema'i grain.
requiresUnderstanding: What dema'i is and why it requires partial tithing
The burden of proof principle in monetary law (hamotzi m'chaveiro)

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi on 'that this one's separation is for holiness': the YK kohein gadol is separated so that his heart will not become arrogant and so that he withdraws from all frivolity. The separation creates an atmosphere of awe (eimah) through being set apart from people.

Rashi: 'and his fellow priests touch him' - meaning: other kohanim are allowed to come into physical contact with the YK kohein gadol during his separation period. His separation is for holiness (attitude), not for purity (physical state).

Rashi: 'and this one's separation is for purity' - the para priest's separation is meant to ensure he is in a state of strict purity, unlike the YK kohein gadol. The reason they are more strict with the para priest is to counter the Sadducean claim that the burning requires sunset (see later).

Rashi: 'his fellow priests do not touch him' - other kohanim are forbidden to touch the para priest during his separation period, because contact could convey impurity and undermine the stringent purity requirement.

Rashi on 'R. Yosei b'R. Chanina raises an objection': granted, days 1, 2, 3 - because perhaps the first day of the separation is day 3 of a prior tumah. Similarly days 5, 6, 7 - because perhaps day 7 of a prior tumah. But day 4 - what possible tumah-counting makes day 4 of the separation either day 3 or day 7 of tumah?

Rashi: 'second [day] perhaps day 3' - on day 2 of the separation, it is possible the priest had tumah contact on the night before the separation began, making day 2 the third day of his tumah. So sprinkling day 2 is warranted.

Rashi: 'third perhaps day 3' - even day 3, perhaps he had tumah contact on day 1 of the separation itself (a brief hour before it began), making day 3 the third day of tumah. So even day 3 merits sprinkling.

Rashi: 'fifth perhaps day 7' - day 5 of the separation could be day 7 of tumah, if the priest had tumah contact during the first day of separation. And similarly day 6 and day 7 cover other possible seventh-day scenarios.

Rashi: 'but the fourth day - why sprinkle at all?' - day 4 cannot be the third day of any tumah (all third-day possibilities are covered by days 1, 2, 3 of separation) and it cannot be the seventh day either (all seventh-day possibilities are covered by days 5, 6, 7).

Rashi: 'is hazaah all seven days possible? But we hold that hazaah is a shvut [and cannot override Shabbat]' - the answer to R. Yosei's challenge is that 'all seven' never truly means seven, because Shabbat always interrupts. So really it is 'seven minus Shabbat.'

Rashi: 'rather, what must you say? Seven minus Shabbat' - the Mishnah's 'all seven' implicitly excludes Shabbat because hazaah is a Rabbinic prohibition that Shabbat overrides. So the actual number of sprinkling days is at most 6.

Rashi: 'here too, seven minus the fourth day' - applying the same logic: just as we say 'seven minus Shabbat' when Shabbat makes hazaah impossible, so too we say 'seven minus day 4' when day 4 is impossible for any logical tumah-counting reason.

Rashi on 'Rava said: therefore' - Rava introduces his resolution of the entire question. The key word 'therefore' (hilkhakh) signals that what follows is the practical ruling that resolves the theoretical problem.

Rashi: 'the High Priest on Yom Kippur, the matter does not depend on us but on the calendar' - the YK kohein gadol must be separated on 3 Tishrei regardless of what day of the week it falls on. We have no control over the starting day.
Rashi: 'on 3 Tishrei he must be separated' - Yom Kippur is 10 Tishrei; the seven-day separation backward puts the start at 3 Tishrei. This is fixed every year by the Torah calendar, not by us.

Rashi: 'whenever 3 Tishrei falls, we separate him' - regardless of the day of the week, the YK kohein gadol is separated on 3 Tishrei. Sometimes his 4th day of separation falls on Shabbat (skipped), sometimes not.

Rashi: 'but the para priest, the matter depends on us' - we control when the para aduma burning ritual takes place. We choose the start date of the para priest's separation, and we can choose it optimally.
Rashi: 'we separate him on the 4th day of the week [Wednesday]' - by beginning the para priest's separation on Wednesday, his 4th day of separation falls on Shabbat, when hazaah is forbidden anyway.

Rashi: 'so that the 4th day [of his separation] will fall on Shabbat' - the para priest separated on Wednesday has: Day 1 = Wednesday, Day 2 = Thursday, Day 3 = Friday, Day 4 = Shabbat (automatically no hazaah), Day 5 = Sunday, Day 6 = Monday, Day 7 = Tuesday.

Rashi: 'so that his 4th day falls on Shabbat' - this scheduling means the one day that cannot logically require hazaah (day 4) is the one day Shabbat prevents it. The impossible day and the forbidden day coincide perfectly.

Rashi: 'so that only day 3 and day 7 [are required]' - with the para priest separated on Wednesday, the Shabbat (his day 4) eliminates the impossible day automatically. The result: his hazaah schedule covers only the days that are logically possible (days 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7) with Shabbat removing the impossible day 4.
Rashi on 'Lishkat Parhedrin etc.' - a baraita: R. Yehuda raises a pointed question: was this room really called Parhedrin? In fact, it was called Lishkat Balvati (Chamber of Nobles/Senators).
Rashi: 'Lishkat Balvati' - the word 'balvati' or 'belutai' comes from the Latin/Greek for senators (bouleutai) - 'like the speech of nobles.' This was the original dignified title for the chamber.
Rashi on 'originally' - meaning: throughout all the days of Shimon HaTzaddik who served for many years and whose era was characterized by dignity and stability, the room was called Lishkat Balvati.

Rashi: 'because they paid money for the High Priesthood' - the Second Temple High Priests would give money to the Hasmonean kings to secure the position of High Priest. This is the corrupt practice that drove the annual rotation.

Rashi: 'and they would replace it every 12 months' - because the kohanim were wicked and did not complete their year, they would die and be replaced. Each new High Priest would tear down the previous one's construction and build his own, so the room was constantly rebuilt and renamed.

Rashi: 'like these parhedrin [Persian governors] who are replaced every 12 months' - the comparison is explicit: just as Persian governors rotate annually, so did the Second Temple High Priests. The comparison is damning.
Rashi: 'therefore they called it Lishkat Parhedrin' - the room acquired its unflattering name as social commentary. The name stuck in the Mishnah, preserving the critique across generations.

Rashi on 'the bakers' (nachtomin): bakers who purchase grain from amei ha'aretz (common people) to bake and sell bread in the market. Sages did not require them to separate maaser sheni of dema'i for Jerusalem. They only required trumat maaser (1/100) and challah to be given to the kohein.

Rashi: 'granted, trumah gedolah - they do not need to [separate] because...' - the baraita explains that trumah gedolah (the large priestly portion, approximately 1/50) does not need to be separated by the baker because a baraita explains why.

Rashi: 'granted, trumah gedolah - no' - the reason bakers do not separate trumah gedolah is explained in the following baraita: Yochanan High Priest sent throughout Israel and found that only trumah gedolah was being universally separated, suggesting it is the only tier that was well-enforced.
Rashi: 'because' - beginning of the reason: because Yochanan sent and found out what was universally practiced. The explanation continues on 9a.

Rashi (continuing on bakers): the full account of Yochanan High Priest's investigation is presented in 9a. Here the Gemara is setting up the context by asking why each tier of tithes does not apply to bakers.

Rashi on bakers and challah: challah is the portion separated from dough and given to the kohein. Unlike maaser sheni, challah must be separated from every baker's dough regardless of dema'i status, because bread production always involves challah obligation.

Rashi: 'bakers who are chaverim (Torah-observant)' - the entire discussion about bakers applies specifically to bakers who are themselves meticulous about Torah observance (chaverim), yet receive grain from amei ha'aretz of uncertain tithe status.

Rashi: 'because they are accustomed to sell constantly' - the reason bakers are treated leniently regarding dema'i maaser sheni is that they sell bread continuously throughout the day. If they had to account for maaser sheni of every batch, the burden would be excessive.

Rashi: 'the Sages were lenient with them' - a deliberate leniency: bakers do not need to separate maaser sheni from dema'i or redeem it. This is a concession to the commercial reality of the bread trade.

Rashi: 'and it is not necessary for them to separate maaser sheni from dema'i to ascend to Jerusalem or redeem it' - the full scope of the leniency: even if the dema'i grain actually had not had maaser sheni separated, the baker is not required to fix this for the customer.

Rashi: 'maaser rishon and maaser ani' - the Gemara asks why bakers also do not need to separate these tithes. Answer: 'the one who claims from another bears the burden of proof' - the Levi or the poor person claiming these tithes must prove the baker's grain was not already tithed.

Rashi: 'since he separated it and removed it from the category of tevel' - once the grain was separated from the larger batch (even as dema'i), the obligation for untithed produce (tevel) prohibition no longer applies strictly. Only a doubtful obligation remains for maaser rishon and maaser ani.

Rashi: 'the Levi and the poor person should bring their proof' - since the doubt is between the baker (who says he tithed) and the claimant (who says he did not), the burden falls on the claimant. The baker therefore owes nothing unless proven otherwise.

Rashi: 'they separate it and ascend and eat it in Jerusalem' - for maaser sheni, which must be eaten in Jerusalem, the bakers do need to separate it from dema'i grain. But the Sages were lenient with bakers specifically for this - the explanation continues on 9a.

Rashi on the final line of 8b: 'the bakers' - this is a concluding reference to the Mishnah being discussed. The explanation of why bakers are exempt from dema'i maaser sheni will be completed when the Gemara resumes on 9a.

Rashi: summary note on the bakers section: bakers are chaverim who buy from amei ha'aretz. The Rabbis exempted them from the full dema'i tithe regime because they sell constantly and the burden would be impossible. Only trumat maaser and challah are required from them.

Rashi (continuation): the phrase 'and they would eat it in Jerusalem' refers to maaser sheni - the second tithe that must physically be consumed in Jerusalem or redeemed. Bakers cannot practically do this for every batch, hence the leniency.

Rashi: 'not all other people' - in contrast to bakers, ordinary individuals who purchase grain from amei ha'aretz must separate maaser sheni from dema'i and either ascend to Jerusalem to eat it or redeem it for money.

Rashi: 'and they separate maaser rishon and maaser ani' - ordinary purchasers must also separate maaser rishon for the Levi and maaser ani for the poor. They separate but do not necessarily give to specific individuals; the obligation is to separate.

Rashi: 'since he is accustomed to sell every hour' - the baker's exemption is grounded in his commercial frequency. He sells bread continuously, making individual tracking of each batch's tithe obligations impossible.

Rashi: 'they [the Sages] were lenient with him, that he does not need to separate maaser sheni from dema'i or redeem it' - the complete statement of the baker's leniency. He is the commercial exception; all other purchasers retain the full obligation.

Rashi on the closing line (transition to 9a): 'because' - this is the connecting word leading into 9a's explanation of why Yochanan High Priest decreed the dema'i rules in the first place. The full baraita about his sending throughout Israel follows on 9a.

glossary.en: A Rabbinic prohibition on Shabbat - an action not forbidden by Torah law but prohibited Rabbinically. Hazaah is a shvut and therefore cannot override Shabbat.
Persian governors who served rotating one-year terms. Used as a comparison for the corrupt Second Temple High Priests who were replaced annually.
Produce of uncertain tithe status bought from an am ha'aretz. The Rabbis required partial separation (trumat maaser, challah) but assumed trumah gedolah had already been taken.
